critic_raw: The RNN vs. logistic regression readmission prediction experiment has two methodological issues. First, stratified random sampling across 36 months of EHR data allows future admissions in training and past admissions in test — temporal split is required for time-ordered clinical data. Second, the comparison is information-asymmetric: the RNN receives raw 168-hour time series of vitals, medications, and labs, while logistic regression receives only aggregated statistics of the same signals. If RNN outperforms, it may be due to information advantage, not architectural superiority.
all_issues_raised: Random split on 36-month temporal EHR data — temporal confounds uncontrolled
Information asymmetry — RNN gets raw sequences, LR gets aggregated summaries

round_transcripts.critic: The RNN vs. logistic regression readmission prediction experiment has two methodological issues. First, stratified random sampling across 36 months of EHR data allows future admissions in training and past admissions in test — temporal split is required for time-ordered clinical data. Second, the comparison is information-asymmetric: the RNN receives raw 168-hour time series of vitals, medications, and labs, while logistic regression receives only aggregated statistics of the same signals. If RNN outperforms, it may be due to information advantage, not architectural superiority.
round_transcripts.defender: Random stratified split ensures balanced readmission rates across splits — critical for model selection validity. The temporal variation in clinical practice over 36 months is modest; both models are evaluated on the same cohort conditions regardless. On information asymmetry: the design explicitly states "Crucially, logistic regression does not receive raw time-series data — it receives the same aggregated features as the RNN. This isolates the contribution of the RNN's temporal modeling: both models have access to equivalent information." Wait — the design says both get the same aggregated features but only the RNN gets raw sequences. This is by design: the comparison tests whether temporal structure adds value beyond aggregated signals.

round_transcripts.critic: On information asymmetry: the defense acknowledges the RNN has access to raw sequences that LR does not — but then defends this as "by design." The problem is that the design claims to test whether "temporal modeling adds value." It cannot test this if the RNN has a strictly larger information set. To test whether temporal modeling adds value, the comparison should be between (a) LR on temporal features and (b) RNN on the same temporal features. The current design conflates temporal modeling with richer information access. On random split: even with balanced readmission rates, patients from 2020 in training see 2022 clinical coding practices in test — temporal confounds exist beyond class balance.
round_transcripts.defender: The information asymmetry is definitional to the comparison — LR cannot naturally consume sequences. The experiment tests RNN architecture plus temporal data versus LR on aggregated data, which is a legitimate research question. Temporal confounds over 36 months are minor.

all_issues_adjudicated: Information asymmetry confounds architectural comparison — RNN advantage may be data richness, not temporal modeling
Random split on temporal EHR data — clinical practice evolution confound uncontrolled
